coredata.dc:title: Green anhydrous synthesis of hydrophilic carbon dots on large-scale and their application for broad fluorescent pH sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Xiaoxia
Yang, Chunlan
Zheng, Baozhan
Dai, Jianyuan
Yan, Lei
Zhuang, Zhenjing
Du, Juan
Guo, Yong
Xiao, Dan
coredata.dc:description: A green and convenient anhydrous method was developed to synthesize hydrophilic carbon dots (H-CDs) on large-scale in this work. During the whole process of preparation, toxicity materials and high pressure conditions were not used, and H-CDs can be formed in several minutes by directly putting glucose powder into hot edible oil, which can be used again. Therefore, it is a green method and very suitable for the industrial preparation of CDs on large-scale. Characterizations demonstrate the H-CDs possess excellent optical properties, higher photostability and very low cytotoxicty. Noticeably, the H-CDs exhibit a distinct pH-sensitive feature, and have been used to construct fluorescent pH sensor with a nice linear relationship in the pH range of 3.0–13.0. To the best of our knowledge, this is the broadest reported pH range based on carbon dots without heteroatom doped. The mechanism of pH-sensitive response was also discussed. The proposed pH sensor has been successfully used for the pH monitoring of environmental water samples and living cells, which demonstrates their applicability in complex matrixes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Anhydrous synthesis
Carbon dots
pH sensor
Environmental samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: pH-dependent intracellular fluorescence imaging and cytotoxicity test
Fluorescent response of H-CDs to pH

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic diagram to illustrate the preparation process of H-CDs by “oil-bath” method and their pH response.
(A) TEM images of H-CDs, (Inset: size distribution of H-CDs). (B) HRTEM images of H-CDs. (C) XRD pattern of the synthesized H-CDs. (D) FT-IR spectra of glucose (curve a) and H-CDs (curve b).
(A) XPS survey scan of H-CDs. The XPS high resolution survey scans of C 1s (B) and O 1s (C) region of H-CDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum (black curve), excitation (green curve) and emission (red curve) spectrum of H-CDs. (Inset: the photographs taken under visible (a) and UV light (b), respectively.) (B) Excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra of H-CDs. (C) The fluorescent intensity of H-CDs under the irradiation of 365nm UV light for 1h. (D) Effects of ionic strength on the fluorescent intensity of H-CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the H-CDs dispersed in PBS solutions with different pH values (3.0–13.0). (B) A linear relationship between the fluorescent intensity and pH (3.0–13.0). (C) The reversible pH-response of the fluorescence behavior of H-CDs between pH 3.0 and 13.0. (D) Response of H-CDs to various metal ions and typical amino acid (10−4 M, from left to right: blank, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, K+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Asp, Cys, Glu, His, Iso, Leu, Ser, Thr, Val).

Laser confocal fluorescence images of Hela cells after incubation with H-CDs at pH 5.0 (A), 7.0 (B), and 9.0 (C) (from left to right: fluorescent image excited with a 405nm laser, brightfield, and overlap of the corresponding bright field image and the fluorescence image, respectively).
Measurement of pH in environmental water samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: pH was measured with a Leici Instruments PHS-3C pH meter.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: pH was measured with the fluorescent pH sensor based on H-CDs in this work.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Sample was from Jinlu Corporation Ltd., Deyang, Sichuan, China.

head.ce:title: Green anhydrous synthesis of hydrophilic carbon dots on large-scale and their application for broad fluorescent pH sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610064, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Sciences, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Criminal Investigation, Southwest University of Political Science and Law, Chongqing 401120, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Southwest University of Political Science and Law

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biomedical Sciences, Huaqiao University, Quanzhou 362021, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A green and convenient anhydrous method was developed in this work, and it is suitable for industrial synthesis of fluorescent hydrophilic carbon dots (H-CDs) on large-scale. Interestingly, the H-CDs exhibit excellent reversible pH-sensitive property in the range of 3.0–13.0.

A green and convenient anhydrous method was developed to synthesize hydrophilic carbon dots (H-CDs) on large-scale in this work. During the whole process of preparation, toxicity materials and high pressure conditions were not used, and H-CDs can be formed in several minutes by directly putting glucose powder into hot edible oil, which can be used again. Therefore, it is a green method and very suitable for the industrial preparation of CDs on large-scale. Characterizations demonstrate the H-CDs possess excellent optical properties, higher photostability and very low cytotoxicty. Noticeably, the H-CDs exhibit a distinct pH-sensitive feature, and have been used to construct fluorescent pH sensor with a nice linear relationship in the pH range of 3.0–13.0. To the best of our knowledge, this is the broadest reported pH range based on carbon dots without heteroatom doped. The mechanism of pH-sensitive response was also discussed. The proposed pH sensor has been successfully used for the pH monitoring of environmental water samples and living cells, which demonstrates their applicability in complex matrixes.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, developing pH probes with higher sensitivity have attracted much attention because the pH level plays pivotal roles in environment and physiological procedures [1,2]. For example, the pH of environment can be affected by the discharge of sewage or the growth of some microbial populations [3], which can arose some environmental and health issues [4]. In addition, the abnormal pH change may also cause physiological dysfunction or diseases [5]. Recently, scientists have found that the abnormal pH can be emerging as a hallmark of cancer [6], which indicates the monitoring of pH may be contributed to the diagnose of cancer. Therefore, it is urgency and important to develop new accurate sensor for the pH monitoring with a wide range response in the environment and/or on the cellular level.

Compared to conventional electrochemical methods, fluorescent pH probes have more benefits in sensitivity, selectivity especially for the intracellular pH monitoring [7–9]. Until to now, many fluorescent probes based on semiconductor quantum dots [10], organic fluorescent dyes [11] and noble-metal nanoclusters [12] have been developed. However, their disadvantages of higher toxicity, lower photostability and complicated synthesis procedures limit their practical applications, especially in the fields of environment [13,14]. Recently, more attention has been attracted to fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) due to their good biocompatibility, low toxicity, excellent optical properties and higher photostability [15–17]. To date, variety of methods have been developed to prepare CDs, such as chemical and electrochemical oxidation [18,19], laser ablation [20], hydrothermal strategies [21], carbonizing organic molecules [22] and microwave methods [23,24]. However, these methods always involve complex synthesis processes, time-consuming, high energy or severe synthetic conditions, which make them not suitable for CDs preparation on large-scale. Therefore, developing more green and simple strategies for large-scale preparation of CDs is still a challenge, and more scientific issues about CDs are still waited for further investigation.

Herein, a green and convenient “oil-bath” method was developed for the synthesis of hydrophilic carbon dots (H-CDs) by directly adding glucose powder into hot edible oil under the mechanical stirring. In the whole process of preparation, only edible materials (glucose powder and edible oil) were used, it is therefore a green method. The reaction is carried out at ambient pressure; the H-CDs could be formed in several minutes and obtained by removing off the oil phase, which can be reused for the next preparation (Scheme 1 ). Therefore, this green and convenient method is suitable for industrial preparation of H-CDs on large-scale. In addition, the obtained H-CDs possess lower cytotoxicity, excellent optical properties. More interestingly, the H-CDs exhibit excellent reversible pH response in acid and basic solutions (Scheme 1), which enables the H-CDs can be used as a potential probe for fluorescent pH sensing of environmental samples and living cells.

Glucose (98%), Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4, NaOH and HCl were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemicals (Chengdu, China). Petroleum ether, NaCl, MgCl2, CaCl2, KCl, FeCl3, NiCl2, MnCl2, CuCl2, Pb(NO3)2 and Hg(NO3)2 were obtained from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Aspartic acid (Asp), cysteine (Cys), glutamic acid (Glu), histidine (His), isoleucine (Iso), leucine (Leu), serine (Ser), threonine (Thr) and valine (Val) were purchased from Alfa Aesar China Chemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Edible oil was from local market. Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St Louis, MO, USA). 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2- yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) was from Solarbio (Beijing, China). All the chemicals were used as received without further purification. The water (≥18.2MΩ) was from a Millipore filtration system and used in all experiments.

H-CDs were synthesized with a green and rapid “oil-bath” method. Briefly, 10g glucose powder was added into 20mL hot edible oil (about 250°C), and the color of the mixture turned dark brown under the mechanical stirring after 5min, which indicated the formation of H-CDs. After cooling down, 50mL water and 20mL petroleum ether were added, and H-CDs could then be dispersed in aqueous phase due to their excellent hydrophilic properties. After three times separation, H-CDs aqueous solution was obtained.

TEM were acquired using a JEOL (JEM-2100) transmission electron microscope operated at 200kV accelerating voltage. The XPS spectra of the synthesized H-CDs were measured on a Kratos AXIS Ultra DLD X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscope using Mg as the excitation source (Kratos, UK). FT-IR was measured using a Thermo Scientific Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer (Sugar Land, TX, USA). UV–vis spectra were recorded by a Hitachi U-2900 Ultraviolet-visible absorption spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan). The fluorescence spectra were obtained on a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). The pH of solutions was measured with a Leici Instruments PHS-3C pH meter (Shanghai, China). Zeta potentials were measured by dynamic laser light scattering (ZS90, Malvern). Cell imaging was performed by a confocal laser scanning microscopy (Nikon, N-SIM).The optical density was read by a BioTek Instrument Power Wave XS2 multi-detection microplate reader (Winooski, VT, USA).

The monitoring of pH was performed at room temperature in 0.1M phosphate buffer solution (PBS) (pH=3.0–13.0). H-CDs solutions with different pH were prepared by adding 50μL H-CDs (10mgmL−1) aqueous solution into 1.45mL PBS with variety of pH. Afterwards, the fluorescence spectra were recorded 1min later in the wavelength range of 380–650nm at an excitation wavelength of 360nm. The fluorescence intensities of H-CDs were used to evaluate the pH value in PBS buffers. All of the measurements were performed in triplicate, and the standard deviation was plotted as the error bar.

For the detection of pH in complex matrixes, three environmental samples, including tap water, pool water and electrolysis brine water, were collected and filtered with a 0.22μm membrane before the pH detection. The obtained water samples were then analyzed using the H-CDs based pH sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH-dependent intracellular fluorescence imaging and cytotoxicity test
ce:section.ce:para: Hela cells were cultured in 1mL Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin streptomycin solution at 37°C in a 5.0% CO2 incubator. Then the cells were incubated with H-CDs (5mgmL−1) for 6h. After that, the cells were washed three times with phosphate buffer saline (PBS containing 1.8mM KH2PO4, 10.1mM Na2HPO4, 2.7mM KCl and 140mM NaCl at pH 7.4). Prior to imaging, cells were treated with 1mL of high-K+ HEPES-buffered solution (125mM KCl, 20mM NaCl, 0.5mM CaCl2, 0.5mM MgCl2, 5mM glucose, and 20mM HEPES) at various pH values (5,7, and 9) in the presence of 5μgmL−1 of nigericin. The cells were incubated for 10min, and the cellular uptake of H-CDs was determined by a confocal laser scanning microscopy at 405nm excitation.

The cytotoxicity of H-CDs was evaluated by a MTT assay. The Hela cells were seeded in a 96 well plates (5000 cells per well) and then cultured in culture media with various concentrations of H-CDs (0.0, 0.0625, 0.125, 0.25, 0.5 and 1.0mgmL−1) for 24h at 37°C in a 5.0% CO2 incubator. Then, the culture medium was replaced by 90μL serum-free appropriate medium and 10μL MTT solution (5.0mgmL−1) and incubated at 37°C for 4h. The reaction solution was carefully aspirated, and 150μL DMSO was added to dissolve the formazan crystals. The optical density was read by a BioTek Instrument Power Wave XS2 multidetection microplate reader (Winooski, VT, USA).

Scheme 1 illustrates the process of H-CDs synthesis with oil-bath strategy. Glucose powder as carbon source was directly added into the hot edible oil (∼250°C) under a soft mechanical stirring, and the color changed quickly from white to dark brown, which indicates the formation of H-CDs, this process can be completed in several minutes. Due to their excellent hydrophilic property, H-CDs can be dispersed in aqueous phase after adding water into the cooled mixture, and H-CDs were obtained by removing off the oil, which can be reused again, therefore, this is a green, simple and fast method, and suitable for the industrial preparation of H-CDs on large-scale. Fig. 1 A shows the TEM image of H-CDs, it can be seen that the synthesized H-CDs are monodispersed spherical nanoparticles with an average size of 2.59nm (Inset of Fig. 1A). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of H-CDs shows well-resolved lattice fringes with an average in-plane lattice spacing of 0.213nm (Fig. 1B), which are very close to the (001) diffraction facets of graphite [25]. The XRD patterns of the as-prepared H-CDs are displayed in Fig. 1C, and the diffraction peak at the range of 21.30–24.25° demonstrates the graphitic structure of H-CDs [26]. Fig. 1D displays the FT-IR spectra of glucose (a) and H-CDs (b), respectively. Comparing to FT-IR spectra of glucose, the obvious broad peak at 3200–3550cm−1 should be attributed to the stretching vibration of OH, which indicates there are plenty of OH on the surface of H-CDs [27]. The peak at 1634cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of CO [28], which should come from the oxidation of OH groups during this synthesis procedure.

The composition of H-CDs was investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). From the XPS survey spectrum of H-CDs (Fig. 2 A), it can be seen that the signals at 286.8eV and 530.6eV indicate the existence of C and O, respectively. Three peaks at 284.6eV, 286.1eV and 287.4eV in the high resolution of C 1s region (Fig. 2B) indicate that carbon is mainly present in three different chemical environments, corresponding to CC/CC, CO and CO, respectively [29]. The O 1s spectrum can also be decomposed into three peaks (Fig. 2C), which are assigned to CO at 531.5eV, CO at 532.3eV and COC at 533.1eV, respectively [30]. These results agree well with the results of FT-IR, and indicate that there are plenty of hydrophilic groups on the surface of carbon dots, which lead to the excellent dispersibility of H-CDs in water.

The optical properties of H-CDs were also investigated. Fig. 3 A shows the UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescent spectrum of H-CDs aqueous solution. Two obvious absorption peaks at 228nm and 282nm in the UV–vis spectrum (black curve) should be attributed to the π-π* and n-π* transitions of H-CDs [31], respectively. With an irradiation by a 365nm UV light, an obvious blue fluorescent emission can be clearly observed by naked eye (inset of Fig. 3Ab). Further, a detailed study on fluorescence properties was carried out by changing the excitation wavelength in the range of 320–480nm. As shown in Fig. 3B, the fluorescent wavelength of H-CDs exhibits an excitation-dependent property, and a maximum emission wavelength of 460nm is obtained with the excitation wavelength of 360nm. In addition, a red-shift emission wavelength from 470 to 550nm is observed by increasing the excitation wavelength from 320nm to 480nm, which is similar to that have been reported for carbon dots prepared by other methods [32]. This excitation-dependent fluorescence feature might be ascribed to the size variation of carbon dots and the distinct emissive traps at their surface [33]. With continuous UV irradiation at 360nm for 1h, no obvious photo-bleaching is observed (Fig. 3C), which indicates the excellent photostability of H-CDs. The effects of ionic strength on the H-CDs were also investigated, and no obvious changes are observed on the photointensity of H-CDs even they are dispersed in an aqueous solution with higher ionic strength of 2.0M NaCl (Fig. 3D). To ensure practical applicability of pH sensor, we also studied the effects of high ionic strength on H-CDs at different pH. As depicted in Fig. S1, no marked changes on fluorescence intensity are observed at different pH values with a high concentration of NaCl (2.0M). These results indicate the H-CDs can resist comparatively high ionic strength and can be applied for the pH monitoring in complex matrixes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent response of H-CDs to pH
ce:section.ce:para: In the process of investigation, we found that the fluorescence intensity decreased with the increasing pH value of solution, which indicates that the synthesized H-CDs possess good pH response property. Therefore, the response of H-CDs to pH was investigated in this work. As illustrated in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescent intensity of H-CDs switched off with the increasing of pH and a nice linear relationship between fluorescent intensity versus pH within 3.0–13.0 is observed (Fig. 4B), which illustrates the H-CDs can be used to construct fluorescent pH probe for quantitative pH measurements. To our knowledge, this is the broadest range for fluorescent pH sensor based on carbon dots without heteroatom doped (Table S1). The reversible property of this pH probe was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 4C, the fluorescent intensity significantly decreases when the pH changes from 3.0 to 13.0 with NaOH, and then restores when reverting the pH from 13.0 to 3.0 with HCl. After 10 times of changing the pH from 3.0 to 13.0 and then to 3.0 again, the fluorescent intensity nearly remains the origin value under the same conditions, which indicates the synthesized H-CDs have excellent reversible pH property. Furthermore, the responses of H-CDs to other common metal ions (Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, K+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Mn2+ and Cu2+) and typical amino acid (Asp, Cys, Glu, His, Iso, Leu, Ser, Thr, Val) were investigated at pH=7.4. From the results, it can be seen that the typical amino acid and metal ions except for Fe3+ at a higher concentration of 10−2 M do not obviously influence the fluorescent intensity of H-CDs (Fig. S2), the fluorescent quenching of H-CDs by Fe3+ should be attributed to the direct interactions between Fe3+ and COOH groups of H-CDs and the inner filter effect of Fe3+ [34,35]. However, all the testing metal ions including Fe3+ and typical amino acid with a lower concentration of 10−4 M do not obviously influence the fluorescent intensity of H-CDs (Fig. 4D), indicating the H-CDs have good selectivity for pH sensing and could be further used for quantitative pH monitoring under physiological and environmental conditions. To illustrate the feasibility of the fluorescent pH sensor in complex matrixes, the proposed pH sensor was employed to measure the pH of real samples, including tap water, pool water and electrolysis brine water. The results (Table 1 ) obtained from present method are comparable to that of obtained from pH meter, which demonstrates that this fluorescent pH sensor based on H-CDs offers an excellent and accurate method for the pH monitoring of real water samples.

The mechanism of H-CDs response to pH was also discussed according to the obtained results. When H-CDs are dispersed in PBS with different pH, a color change from light yellow to dark yellow is observed as the pH increases from 3.0 to 13.0 (Fig. S3A), and the fluorescent intensity of H-CDs decreases at the same conditions (Fig. S3B). To get a better understanding of the possible mechanism, some of the characterizations (TEM of H-CDs at different pH values and Zeta potentials) have also been carried out. As shown in Fig. S4, the average size of the H-CDs at pH=4.0, 7.0, 10.0 and 13.0 is 3.22nm, 2.59nm, 3.03nm and 2.52nm, respectively. There is no obvious change for the size with the increase of pH, which indicates the fluorescent quenching of the as-prepared H-CDs at high pH might not be attributed to the aggregation of nanoparticles [36]. The zeta potentials of H-CDs at different pH also were measured, as depicted in Fig. S5, the zeta potential changes from positive to negative as the increasing pH from 2.0 to 12.0, which is consistent with the phenomenon of pH-induced deprotonation of the surface functional group. Fig. S6 shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of H-CDs at pH=3.0 and 13.0, respectively, it can be seen that the absorption of H-CDs at pH=13.0 is larger than that of at pH=3.0 in the band of 230–550nm, which should also be attributed to the deprotonation of the surface functional groups [37]. The deprotonation may cause electrostatic doping/charging to CDs and shift the Fermi level [29], and therefore affect the fluorescent properties of CDs. At lower pH, the strong vibrational coupling of the OH functional groups, caused by H-bonding effects, can lead to the energy level broadening and increase the conformational rigidity of H-CDs [38,39], therefore, the fluorescent intensity is strong at acid conditions (Scheme 1). However, the eliminated H-bonding effect upon deprotonation at basic conditions may lead to the reduced vibrational coupling and more discrete energy levels [38,40], which results in the decrease of fluorescence for H-CDs (Scheme 1). Based on the above observations, we conclude that the variation of fluorescent intensity of H-CDs under different pH should be attributed to the reversible protonation/deprotonation of H-CDs in acidic and alkaline media, which is consistent with the previous reported work [41].

The H-CDs have important potential applications in biological and environmental fields due to their good biocompatibility and non-toxicity. MTT assay was performed to evaluate the cytotoxicity of H-CDs before they were applied as fluorescent probes for bioimaging and pH sensing, and the results are shown in Fig. S7. The viability of the cells exceeds 98% even incubated with H-CDs at a high concentration of 1mgmL−1 for 24h, which demonstrates the H-CDs prepared by this “oil-bath” method exhibit very low cytotoxicity to the cells.

The laser confocal fluorescence images of Hela cells were carried out to further assess the potential application of the H-CDs for imaging and pH sensing in living cells. As shown in Fig. 5 , blue fluorescence emissions from H-CDs could be observed from HeLa cells at a 405nm excitation. The fluorescent spots are found in the cell membrane and cytoplasmic area of the cell, indicating that the H-CDs can easily penetrate into the cell. Furthermore, H-CDs-stained HeLa cells exhibit strong blue fluorescence at pH 5.0 (Fig. 5A), whereas the fluorescent brightness obviously reduces as the pH increases (Fig. 5B and C). These results demonstrate the potential application of this fluorescent probe based on H-CDs for the pH sensing in biological matrix.

In summary, a green and convenient “oil-bath” method was developed for the synthesis of H-CDs with large-scale. It is a green method because only edible materials (glucose and edible oil) were used during the whole process, and the convenient preparation and separation of H-CDs make this method suitable for industrial synthesis on large-scale. The synthesized H-CDs exhibit excellent optical properties, low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility and photostability. More interestingly, the H-CDs have a good fluorescent response to pH with a broad range of 3.0–13.0, and it has been successfully applied as a fluorescent sensor for the pH monitoring of environmental samples and Hela cells. This study not only provides us an attractive strategy for carbon dots preparation on large-scale, but also opens up new avenues to explore the use of carbon dots with lower cytotoxicity as a fluorescent probe to monitor the pH value of environmental samples and biological matrix.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (No. 21407109, 51401174, 21307034) and the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (No. cstcjjA00026).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.08.101.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spatially and functionally distinct Ca2+ stores in sarcoplasmic and endoplasmic reticulum
Localized intracellular pH measurement using a ratiometric photoinduced electron-transfer-based nanosensor
A convenient purification method for silver nanoclusters and its applications in fluorescent pH sensors for bacterial monitoring
Nitrogen-rich functional groups carbon nanoparticles based fluorescent pH sensor with broad-range responding for environmental and live cells applications
A single probe for imaging and biosensing of pH, Cu2+ ions, and pH/Cu2+ in live cells with ratiometric fluorescence signals
Dysregulated pH: a perfect storm for cancer progression
Solution-gated epitaxial graphene as pH sensor
Gold nanocluster-based fluorescence biosensor for targeted imaging in cancer cells and ratiometric determination of intracellular pH
Graphite screen-printed electrodes applied for the accurate and reagentless sensing of pH
A simple pH fluorescent probe based on new fluorophore indolizine for imaging of living cells
Green synthesis of fluorescent copper nanoclusters for reversible pH-sensors
Taking advantage of luminescent lanthanide ions
Recent advances in the chemistry of lanthanide-doped upconversion nanocrystals
Intercrossed carbon nanorings with pure surface states as low-cost and environment-friendly phosphors for white-light-emitting diodes
Carbon quantum dots and their applications
Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence
An electrochemical avenue to green-luminescent graphene quantum dots as potential electron-acceptors for photovoltaics
Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
Hg(II) sensing based on functionalized carbon dots obtained by direct laser ablation
Facile synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots and application for Fe(III) ions detection and cell imaging
Deep ultraviolet photoluminescence of water-soluble self-passivated graphene quantum dots
Microwave assisted one-step green synthesis of cell-permeable multicolor photoluminescent carbon dots without surface passivation reagents
Microwave synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles with electrochemiluminescence properties
Drug-derived bright and color-tunable N-doped carbon dots for cell imaging and sensitive detection of Fe3+ in living cells
Microwave assisted one-pot synthesis of graphene quantum dots as highly sensitivefl uorescent probes for detection of iron ions and pH value
Intrinsically fluorescent carbon dots with tunable emission derived from hydrothermal treatment of glucose in the presence of monopotassium phosphate
On-Off-On fluorescent carbon dot nanosensor for recognition of chromium(VI) and ascorbic acid based on the inner filter effect
One-pot green synthesis of optically pH-sensitive carbon dots with upconversion luminescence

Hydrothermal treatment of grass: a low-cost, green route to nitrogen-doped, carbon-rich, photoluminescent polymer nanodots as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for label-free detection of Cu(II) ions
Energy-level structure of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
Controllable synthesis of green and blue fluorescent carbon nanodots for pH and Cu2+ sensing in living cells
Membrane analysis with amphiphilic carbon dots
One-step synthesis of self-doped carbon dots with highly photoluminescence as multifunctional biosensors for detection of iron ions and pH
Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles: a low-temperature trypsin-assisted preparation and Fe3+ sensing
A hydrothermal route to water-stable luminescent carbon dots as nanosensors for pH and temperature
Polyethylenimine-derived fluorescent nonconjugated polymer dots with reversible dual-signal pH response and logic gate operation
pH-elicited luminescence functionalities of carbon dots: mechanistic insights
Fluorescence modulation in anion sensing by introducing intramolecular H-bonding interactions in host-guest adducts
Ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor based on water soluble carbon nanodots with multiple sensing capacities
The production of pH-sensitive photoluminescent carbon nanoparticles by the carbonization of polyethylenimine and their use for bioimaging

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xiaoxia Liu received her BSc degree in chemical biology in 2015 from Sichuan Agricultural University. She is currently working toward her MSc degree in analytical chemistry under the supervision of Prof. Yong Guo at Sichuan University. Her research focuses on biosensor and bioanalytical chemistry.

Chunlan Yang received her BSc degree in bioscience in 2015 from China West Normal University. She is currently working toward her MSc degree in cell biology under the supervision of associate professor Chuanfang Wu at Sichuan University. Her research focuses on the roles of Long non coding RNA in cancer.

Baozhan Zheng received his BSc degree in environmental engineering in 2004 from Henan Normal University, and obtained his MSc in 2007 and PhD in 2010 in applied chemistry from Sichuan University. He is working at department of chemistry in Sichuan University since 2010 and become an associate professor since 2015. He visited Hong Kong Baptist University as an exchange student and postdoctoral fellow in 2009 and 2014, respectively. His research focuses on nano-materials, chemosensor and biosensors.

Jianyuan Dai received his BS degree in 2002 from Chongqing Normal University, and obtained his MS in 2005 in analytical chemistry from Southwest University, then obtained his PhD in 2008 in analytical chemistry from Nanjing University. He is working at department of chemistry in Sichuan University was an associate professor since 2016. He visited California University at Santa Barbara as postdoctoral fellow from 2010 to 2011. His research focuses on DNA nanomaterials and biosensors.

Lei Yan received her BSc degree in pharmaceutical engineering in 2004 from Chengdu University of traditional Chinese Medicine, obtained her MSc in 2007 in pharmaceutical chemistry from Chongqing University, and received her PhD in 2011 in analytical chemistry from Sichuan University. She is working at school of criminal investigation in Southwest University of political science and law since 2012. She visited Hong Kong Baptist University as an exchange student in 2009. Her research focuses on nano-materials, chemosensor and forensic science.

Zhenjing Zhuang received her BSc degree at chemistry in 2002 from Fujian Normal University, and obtained his MSc in 2005 at Analytical Chemistry and PhD in 2008 at a applied chemistry from Sichuan University. She is working at school of biomedical science in Huaqiao University and become an associate professor since 2012. She visited Temple University as an visiting scholar in 2014. Her research focuses on chemical and biosensors.

Juan Du received her PhD degree in physical chemistry in 2003 from Sichuan University. She has been an associate professor in 2006 and a professor in 2015 at Sichuan University. Her current research interests are spectrum analysis and biosensors.

Yong Guo received his BSc in chemistry in 1991, MSc in 1994 and PhD in 2003 in physical chemistry from Sichuan University and was a professor since 2008 at Sichuan University. His current research interests are molecular structure and spectrum, nano-material and analytical chemistry.

Dan Xiao received his BSc in chemistry in 1982, MSc in 1994 and PhD in 1999 in analytical chemistry from Hunan University and was an associate professor since 1996 and professor since 2000 at Hunan University, respectively. He moved to Sichuan University as professor of analytical chemistry in 2002. His current research interests are analytical chemistry, biosensor, chemosensor, nano-material and electrophoresis.
